past_medical_history: Atrial fibrillation, diagnosed 3 years ago, not on anticoagulation due to prior fall-related concern
Hypertension, diagnosed 12 years ago, on medication
COPD, diagnosed 8 years ago, uses albuterol inhaler PRN
Obstructive sleep apnea, diagnosed 5 years ago, non-adherent with CPAP
Remote history of dental extraction complicated by dry socket, 2 years ago
family_history: Mother had a stroke at age 80
Father had coronary artery disease, died of myocardial infarction at age 68

social_history.smoking: Former smoker, 30 pack-year history, quit 6 years ago
social_history.alcohol: Occasional glass of wine, 1-2 per week
social_history.living_situation: Lives alone in a single-story apartment, daughter lives nearby
social_history.exercise: Walks around the block 2-3 times per week, limited by knee pain

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, and time. Mildly anxious due to pain. Speech fluent without dysarthria. Language comprehension and repetition intact. Naming intact for high and low-frequency objects. Follows three-step commands bilaterally. No evidence of neglect on double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, reactive to light. Visual fields full to confrontation. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. No facial asymmetry at rest. Mild tenderness to palpation over the left mandibular region. No facial sensory deficit to light touch or pinprick in V1, V2, or V3 distributions. Jaw opening and closing without clicking, grinding, or deviation. Hearing grossly intact. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Upper extremities: 5/5 strength bilaterally in deltoid, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors, grip, and finger abductors. Lower extremities: 5/5 strength bilaterally in hip flexion, knee extension, ankle dorsiflexion, and plantar flexion. No pronator drift. No fasciculations or atrophy.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick intact in all extremities. Proprioception intact at great toes bilaterally. No sensory extinction on double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 2+ bilaterally, Achilles 1+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose intact bilaterally. Heel-to-shin intact bilaterally. No dysdiadochokinesia. Rapid alternating movements symmetric.
neurological_exam.gait: Steady gait, mild antalgic component favoring right knee. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: NIHSS score: 0. No meningismus. No carotid bruits appreciated on initial auscultation. Jaw opening against resistance is mildly limited by pain on the left but without true weakness. Temporal arteries palpable bilaterally without tenderness.

patient.chief_complaint: Intermittent left-sided jaw and lower face pain, sharp and lightning-like, recurrent over two days
patient.history_present_illness: Mrs. Dorothy Kowalski is a 74-year-old right-handed Caucasian woman with a history of atrial fibrillation (not on anticoagulation), hypertension, COPD, and obstructive sleep apnea who presents to the emergency department for the second time in two days with worsening left-sided jaw and lower face pain. She first presented two days ago reporting that she had been awakened from sleep that morning by a sudden onset of sharp, lightning-like pain in the left jaw and lower face. The pain was intermittent, lasting seconds to minutes at a time, and she described it as unlike anything she had experienced before. At that initial visit, her neurological examination was unremarkable, and she was assessed for possible trigeminal neuralgia. Neurology was consulted and recommended outpatient dental evaluation and neurology follow-up. Her apixaban, which she had previously been prescribed but had stopped taking several months ago, was restarted. She was discharged home with instructions to follow up within one week. Today, she returns because the left jaw and lower face pain has become moderate to severe and is now nearly continuous rather than intermittent. She took acetaminophen at home without relief. She describes the pain as originating deep in the left jaw, occasionally radiating toward the left ear and left side of the neck. She denies any headache, visual changes, limb weakness, numbness, tingling, difficulty speaking, or swallowing problems. She has not had any fever, night sweats, or weight loss. She denies any recent dental procedures or trauma to the face. She does note that she has been feeling more fatigued over the past week, which she attributes to poor sleep from her untreated sleep apnea. On this return visit, the emergency physician is concerned about the refractory nature of the pain and considers a broader differential diagnosis beyond trigeminal neuralgia, including temporal arteritis, acute coronary syndrome presenting as referred jaw pain, and vascular pathology such as carotid artery dissection. IV methylprednisolone 250 mg is administered empirically for possible temporal arteritis, and ketorolac is given for analgesia. The pain remains refractory, and IV fentanyl is administered for additional relief.

findings.mass_effect: No mass effect. No midline shift.

findings.location: Bilateral periventricular and deep subcortical white matter

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Multifocal punctate and early confluent periventricular white matter hyperintensities

signal_characteristics.T1: Sulcal prominence and mild ventricular enlargement, appropriate to mildly advanced for age

additional_observations: No evidence of hemorrhage on susceptibility-weighted imaging
Normal appearance of bilateral temporal bones and mastoids
MRA of circle of Willis: patent bilateral ACA, MCA, and PCA. No aneurysm or stenosis identified
mri.impression: No evidence of acute infarction, hemorrhage, or mass lesion. Moderate chronic small vessel ischemic disease (Fazekas grade 2). Mild generalized cerebral volume loss. MRA of the circle of Willis is unremarkable. The negative initial brain MRI does not exclude a cervical vascular etiology for the patient's symptoms. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
No acute ST segment elevation or depression
Nonspecific T-wave flattening in leads V4-V6
ecg.interpretation: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response at approximately 105 bpm. No acute ischemic changes. Nonspecific T-wave flattening in lateral precordial leads. Left atrial abnormality.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Glucose 108 mg/dL (H), ESR 34 mm/hr (H), CRP 0.8 mg/dL (H), TSH 5.8 mIU/L (H).

findings.location: Left internal carotid artery, cervical segment, beginning approximately 2 cm distal to the bifurcation and extending over a 3 cm segment

signal_characteristics.CTA: Eccentric mural thickening with intramural hematoma causing moderate luminal narrowing (approximately 60% stenosis). Crescent-shaped mural thickening with intimal flap visible. No complete occlusion. Distal ICA reconstitutes with normal caliber at the skull base.
signal_characteristics.Vessel_wall: Classic flame-shaped tapering of the left ICA lumen at the dissection origin

findings.borders: Well-defined dissection flap with smooth tapering proximally and distally

signal_characteristics.CTA: Scattered calcified plaque at bilateral carotid bulbs without hemodynamically significant stenosis (less than 30% bilaterally)

additional_observations: Right ICA is patent with normal caliber throughout its course
Bilateral vertebral arteries are patent with normal caliber
No pseudoaneurysm at the dissection site
Mild atherosclerotic calcification of the aortic arch
output.impression: Left internal carotid artery dissection in the cervical segment with intramural hematoma, intimal flap, and approximately 60% luminal narrowing. No complete occlusion. No intracranial large vessel occlusion or acute embolic complication identified on this study. Mild bilateral carotid bifurcation atherosclerotic disease without significant stenosis. This is a critical finding that explains the patient's refractory left-sided jaw and face pain (referred pain from the ipsilateral ICA dissection). Carotid dissection carries a significant risk of thromboembolic stroke. Clinical correlation recommended.

signal_characteristics.Flow_pattern: High-resistance biphasic flow pattern in the mid-cervical left ICA, atypical for this location. Dampened and tardus-parvus waveform distally, suggesting proximal flow-limiting pathology.
signal_characteristics.B_mode: Echogenic material within the vessel wall with narrowed residual lumen. No classic atherosclerotic plaque morphology.

additional_observations: Bilateral vertebral arteries patent with antegrade flow
Common carotid arteries demonstrate mildly increased intima-media thickness bilaterally
No evidence of external carotid artery abnormality
output.impression: Abnormal left ICA flow with elevated velocities, high-resistance waveform, and echogenic mural material — findings suspicious for carotid dissection rather than atherosclerotic disease. Normal right ICA. Patent bilateral vertebral arteries. Clinical correlation recommended.

Transthoracic Echocardiogram.value: Intact, no PFO or ASD on color Doppler
Not well visualized on TTE; TEE may be considered if thrombus exclusion is needed

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Left Atrial Volume Index 38 mL/m2 (H), Left Ventricular Wall Thickness Mild concentric LVH (septal thickness 1.2 cm)  (H), Diastolic Function Grade I diastolic dysfunction (impaired relaxation)  (H), Mitral Valve Mild-to-moderate mitral regurgitation  (H), Aortic Valve Mild aortic sclerosis without stenosis  (H).

Temporal Artery Biopsy.value: No evidence of vasculitis. Normal arterial wall architecture without granulomatous inflammation, giant cells, or intimal hyperplasia. Mild age-related arteriosclerotic changes only.

output.query: carotid artery dissection jaw pain facial pain atypical presentation diagnosis

results.title: Cervical Artery Dissection: A Review of Clinical Features, Diagnosis, and Management
results.key_finding: Carotid artery dissection can present with ipsilateral head, face, or neck pain in up to 65% of cases. Pain may precede ischemic events by hours to days. Isolated pain without neurological deficits is an under-recognized presentation.

results.title: Carotid Dissection Presenting as Facial Pain: A Case Series and Review
results.key_finding: Lower facial and jaw pain from carotid dissection results from compression or stretching of the pericarotid sympathetic and trigeminal nerve fibers. Pain can mimic trigeminal neuralgia, TMJ dysfunction, or dental pain.

results.title: Clinical Practice and Cases: Carotid Dissection Mimicking Trigeminal Neuralgia
results.journal: Clinical Practice and Cases in Emergency Medicine
results.key_finding: Carotid dissection should be considered in the differential of acute-onset unilateral face or jaw pain, especially when refractory to standard analgesia. CTA of the head and neck is the diagnostic study of choice. The absence of focal neurological deficits does not exclude dissection.

output.summary: Carotid artery dissection (CAD) is an important but frequently missed cause of unilateral head, face, and neck pain. The pain results from stretching of nociceptive fibers in the adventitia or pericarotid plexus. CAD accounts for up to 25% of strokes in patients under 50, but can occur at any age. Key clinical clues include: unilateral pain in the face, jaw, neck, or head that is refractory to standard analgesics; ipsilateral Horner syndrome (present in only 30-50% of cases); and a temporal pattern where pain precedes ischemic events by hours to weeks. The absence of neurological deficits at presentation does not exclude the diagnosis, as many patients present with isolated pain before developing stroke symptoms. CTA of the head and neck is the recommended initial diagnostic study, with sensitivity exceeding 95% for dissection. Early diagnosis is critical because anticoagulation or antiplatelet therapy can prevent thromboembolic complications.

interactions: Aspirin 81 mg: Increased bleeding risk with concomitant anticoagulant and antiplatelet therapy. In the context of carotid dissection with atrial fibrillation, anticoagulation alone is typically sufficient. Aspirin should generally be discontinued unless a specific indication for dual therapy exists.
warnings: Patient was recently restarted on apixaban after a period of non-adherence. Ensure consistent dosing and patient education.
Avoid concurrent NSAIDs (ketorolac was administered in the ED — this is short-term only and should be discontinued once anticoagulation is established)
Monitor for signs of bleeding, especially given the acute dissection with intramural hematoma
If the patient develops neurological deficits suggesting acute stroke, the timing of anticoagulation relative to infarct size will need reassessment
output.formulary_status: Preferred formulary agent for non-valvular atrial fibrillation
alternatives: Heparin infusion (for acute-phase anticoagulation in dissection, with transition to oral agent)
Warfarin (target INR 2-3, requires bridging with heparin)
Dual antiplatelet (aspirin + clopidogrel) — some evidence supports equivalence to anticoagulation in cervical dissection (CADISS trial)

output.query: giant cell arteritis temporal arteritis diagnostic criteria ESR CRP jaw claudication

results.title: 2018 Update of the EULAR Recommendations for the Management of Large Vessel Vasculitis
results.key_finding: In suspected GCA, ESR >50 mm/hr and CRP >2.45 mg/dL have high sensitivity. Normal inflammatory markers significantly reduce but do not completely exclude the diagnosis. Temporal artery biopsy remains the gold standard.

results.title: Giant Cell Arteritis: ACR/EULAR Classification Criteria
results.key_finding: The 2022 classification criteria emphasize age >50, new-onset localized headache, temporal artery abnormality, ESR >50, and biopsy findings. Jaw claudication (pain with chewing that resolves with rest) is a classic feature, distinct from constant jaw pain.

output.summary: Giant cell arteritis (GCA) typically presents in patients over 50 with new-onset headache, jaw claudication, visual symptoms, and markedly elevated inflammatory markers (ESR usually >50, often >80-100 mm/hr; CRP >2.45 mg/dL). In this patient, the ESR of 34 and CRP of 0.8 are below typical diagnostic thresholds for GCA. Additionally, the pain character (constant, sharp, lightning-like) differs from classic jaw claudication (fatigue-like pain with sustained chewing that resolves with rest). While GCA cannot be completely excluded based on inflammatory markers alone, the clinical picture is atypical for GCA, and alternative diagnoses should be strongly considered.

findings.location: Left anterior circulation — small focus in the left frontal operculum and adjacent insular cortex, within the left MCA territory

signal_characteristics.DWI: Hyperintense signal consistent with restricted diffusion
signal_characteristics.ADC: Corresponding hypointensity confirming true restricted diffusion
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Faintly hyperintense, suggesting very early infarct (likely within 6-12 hours)
signal_characteristics.T2: Subtle hyperintensity in the left frontal operculum

findings.mass_effect: No mass effect. No midline shift.
findings.borders: Irregular borders, consistent with embolic infarct pattern

findings.location: Bilateral periventricular and deep subcortical white matter

additional_observations: New finding compared to prior MRI obtained 2 days ago
No additional areas of restricted diffusion
MRA of circle of Willis: left MCA patent, no large vessel occlusion
output.impression: This was NOT present on the initial MRI obtained 2 days ago. No hemorrhagic transformation. Unchanged chronic small vessel ischemic disease. This underscores the critical importance of anticoagulation in this patient. If the patient is not yet anticoagulated, this should be initiated urgently. Clinical correlation recommended.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Ischemic stroke secondary to left internal carotid artery dissection, presenting as isolated refractory jaw and facial pain prior to cerebral infarction

differential.key_distinguishing: Paroxysmal lightning-like pain is suggestive, but refractory nature to treatment and unilateral jaw pain without classic trigger zones argues against it. CTA reveals dissection as the cause.
Age and jaw pain raise concern, but ESR only mildly elevated (34), CRP low (0.8), no temporal artery tenderness, pain character differs from jaw claudication, and biopsy is negative.
Normal troponin, no ECG ischemic changes, no chest pain or dyspnea. Cardiac etiology effectively excluded.
No clicking, grinding, or deviation on jaw opening. Pain is sharp and lightning-like rather than dull aching typical of TMJ.
No dental examination abnormalities, no recent procedures. Pain character is atypical for dental origin.
No vesicular rash, no dermatomal distribution. Pain preceded by days without skin findings makes this unlikely.

optimal_actions.action: Perform a thorough neurological examination and assess vital signs, noting normal neurological exam but hypertension and tachycardia
optimal_actions.expected_finding: NIHSS 0, normal neurological exam, BP 151/101, HR 105 (tachycardia consistent with AF)

optimal_actions.action: Obtain 12-lead ECG to evaluate for acute coronary syndrome and assess cardiac rhythm
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response, no acute ischemic changes — excludes ACS but confirms AF as a thromboembolic risk factor

optimal_actions.action: Obtain stat labs including CBC, BMP, inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP), troponin, and coagulation studies
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal troponin excludes ACS; mildly elevated ESR and CRP insufficient for GCA; elevated TSH is incidental

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain MRI with DWI to evaluate for acute intracranial pathology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No acute infarction on initial MRI — chronic small vessel disease only. Negative brain MRI does not exclude cervical vascular pathology.

optimal_actions.action: Given refractory unilateral face/jaw pain in a patient with vascular risk factors and AF, expand the differential to include carotid dissection and obtain CTA of the head and neck
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left ICA dissection with intramural hematoma, intimal flap, and 60% luminal narrowing — the critical diagnostic finding

optimal_actions.action: Initiate anticoagulation for left ICA dissection in the setting of existing atrial fibrillation (both indications converge on anticoagulation)
Admit to stroke unit for close neurological monitoring given risk of thromboembolic stroke from dissection
Obtain follow-up brain MRI to evaluate for interval embolic events from the dissection
optimal_actions.expected_finding: New small acute infarct in left frontal operculum, confirming thromboembolic complication of the dissection

optimal_actions.action: Order transthoracic echocardiogram to evaluate cardiac structure given known AF
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Dilated left atrium, LVH, diastolic dysfunction — confirms substrate for thromboembolism from AF

optimal_actions.action: Search medical literature on carotid dissection presenting as jaw pain to confirm diagnostic reasoning
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Literature confirms that ipsilateral face/jaw pain is a well-described presentation of carotid dissection

optimal_actions.action: Plan follow-up CTA or MRA in 3-6 months to assess dissection healing, optimize BP control, and ensure adherence to anticoagulation

critical_actions: Recognize that refractory unilateral jaw/face pain, especially in a patient with vascular risk factors, warrants consideration of carotid artery dissection
Obtain CTA of the head and neck when pain is refractory and initial brain imaging is negative — do not stop at a negative brain MRI
Initiate anticoagulation promptly once carotid dissection is diagnosed to prevent thromboembolic stroke
Recognize that the initial normal neurological exam does not exclude a serious vascular pathology — carotid dissection often presents with isolated pain before stroke occurs
Assess and manage the atrial fibrillation, which compounds thromboembolic risk in this patient
contraindicated_actions: Discharging the patient with refractory pain attributed to trigeminal neuralgia without vascular imaging
Initiating carbamazepine for presumed trigeminal neuralgia without excluding vascular pathology, potentially masking the sentinel pain of dissection
Administering IV tPA without confirmed acute ischemic infarction on brain imaging
Performing aggressive cervical manipulation or chiropractic maneuvers that could worsen the dissection
Delaying anticoagulation once carotid dissection is confirmed, allowing progression to stroke
key_reasoning_points: Jaw and lower face pain can be the sole initial manifestation of carotid artery dissection due to compression or stretching of pericarotid nerve fibers — this is an under-recognized presentation
A negative initial brain MRI does not exclude carotid dissection; the pathology is in the cervical vasculature, not the brain parenchyma. CTA of the head AND neck is needed.
The mildly elevated ESR (34) and CRP (0.8) are red herrings — they are nonspecific and below the thresholds typically associated with giant cell arteritis (ESR >50, CRP >5)
The elevated TSH (5.8) is an incidental finding representing subclinical hypothyroidism and should not distract from the acute vascular diagnosis
Refractory pain despite methylprednisolone, ketorolac, and fentanyl is a clinical red flag that should prompt reconsideration of the diagnosis rather than escalation of analgesia alone
This patient has two independent indications for anticoagulation — atrial fibrillation and carotid dissection — both converging on the same therapeutic intervention

The normal neurological examination on initial presentation represents the 'warning pain' phase of carotid dissection, which can precede ischemic stroke by hours to weeks. Early diagnosis at this stage is potentially stroke-preventing.
Pain from carotid dissection can closely mimic trigeminal neuralgia, temporal arteritis, dental pain, and TMJ dysfunction — a broad differential and targeted vascular imaging are essential

metadata.difficulty_description: Moderate — carotid dissection presenting as isolated refractory jaw pain with initially normal neurological exam and negative brain MRI. Mild inflammatory marker elevation and elevated TSH serve as red herrings. Diagnosis requires recognizing the need for cervical vascular imaging despite negative intracranial imaging.
